case_name: EXN v Alder Hey Children's NHS Foundation Trust [2021] EWHC 2989 (QB) (08 November 2021)

evidence_index.text: On 19 September 2009, the Claimant (who was just 17 months old) suffered a focal seizure which affected the right side of his face.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He was sent home and, despite re-attendance, was not diagnosed with the virus HSV until 24 September 2009.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Subsequently, the Defendant admitted breach of duty. Subject to one contested point, the Defendant also admitted causation of loss and damage.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Claimant suffers from severe learning difficulties, mild right hemiplegia, severe drug-resistant multifocal epilepsy, autism and severe behavioural problems with violent outbursts.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: I am satisfied that the proposed settlement is a sensible settlement from the Claimant's point of view.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Mr Gimlette has also informed the Court that a letter of apology was sent to the Claimant's parents in this case in September and that an apology has been given on behalf the Defendant by its Leading Counsel.
evidence_index.speaker: Defendant's counsel

initial_state.chief_complaint: Focal seizure affecting the right side of the face
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient suffered a focal seizure affecting the right side of the face and was taken to Alder Hey Children's Hospital. While in the A&E department, he suffered a second focal seizure.
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Seizure disorder - differential includes viral infection

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to A&E with seizure activity

timeline_phases.name: Diagnostic Workup
timeline_phases.description: Patient observed in A&E, second seizure occurs

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in A&E with recurrent seizures
decision_points.prompt: What diagnostic test should be ordered to identify the cause of seizures?

options.clinical_reasoning: Early identification of HSV can prevent severe complications
Failed to identify underlying viral cause

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant discharged patient without further testing

expected_action_court.description: HSV PCR test should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Early testing for HSV in seizure cases is standard

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to test for HSV led to delayed diagnosis and treatment
explanation.what_should_have_happened: HSV PCR test should have been conducted to confirm diagnosis
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Breach of duty admitted by Defendant

patient_outcome.description: Patient sustained catastrophic brain injuries, leading to profound impairments and intractable epilepsy
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Severe learning difficulties, mild right hemiplegia, severe drug-resistant multifocal epilepsy, autism, and severe behavioral problems

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to diagnose HSV
Causation established - earlier diagnosis would have prevented severe outcomes

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Early diagnosis and treatment of HSV would have prevented severe brain injury

factual_timeline.event: Patient suffered a focal seizure and was taken to Alder Hey Children's Hospital

court_findings.legal_significance: Breach of duty and causation admitted
